research.interface: Salesforce GraphQL API, POST /services/data/v67.0/graphql (Summer '26, API version 67.0)

official_sources.title: Requests and Responses | Schema Structure | GraphQL API
official_sources.used_for: endpoint URL form https://{MyDomainName}.my.salesforce.com/services/data/v{version}/graphql; POST JSON body parts query (required string), operationName, variables, extensions; response sections data, errors, extensions

official_sources.title: Query Objects | Query Records | GraphQL API
official_sources.used_for: uiapi wrapper with a query RecordQuery root; object fields returning connection types (AccountConnection); RecordEdge with node and cursor; field-value types where every accessible field exposes a value field; Record interface scalars such as Id

official_sources.title: Paginate with Record Connections | Query Records | GraphQL API
official_sources.used_for: first argument bounding page size (default 10), after argument taking a cursor, pageInfo.endCursor continuation, cursor-per-edge, and passing endCursor as after while hasNextPage is true

official_sources.title: Status Codes and Error Responses | Get Started | GraphQL API
official_sources.used_for: GraphQL API returns 200 OK even when the request contains errors; 401 for expired/invalid session; 404 when a non-graphql path is used; GraphQLErrorRepresentation ErrorType values InvalidSyntax, ValidationError, DataFetchingException, OperationNotSupported, ExecutionAborted

official_sources.title: What is GraphQL API? | Get Started | GraphQL API
official_sources.used_for: the schema exposes UI API enabled sObjects honoring the context user's object- and field-level security, so two users can see different schemas

research.language_rationale: The consuming revenue-reporting service is a JVM batch job; Salesforce documents the GraphQL API as a raw HTTP POST protocol without an official Java SDK, so java.net.http.HttpClient against the documented endpoint is the credible zero-dependency integration surface.

verified_facts: The only GraphQL endpoint is POST {instance}/services/data/v{version}/graphql; requests are JSON with a query string plus optional operationName, variables and extensions; any other path under the endpoint answers 404 (only the graphql endpoint is supported).

Record queries nest under uiapi.query.<ObjectApiName>; the result is a connection with edges (each edge carrying node and cursor), pageInfo, and totalCount; every accessible record field is a field-value object exposing value (Id is a plain scalar from the Record interface).

Pagination is relay-style: pass first to bound the page (server default 10), read pageInfo.hasNextPage and pageInfo.endCursor, and pass endCursor as after on the next request; cursors are opaque strings.

GraphQL execution failures are reported in the errors section of a 200 OK response and can accompany partial data; error entries carry message and an ErrorType classification among InvalidSyntax, ValidationError, DataFetchingException, OperationNotSupported, ExecutionAborted (this seed's fake, like the graphql-java engine underneath the endpoint, carries the classification in extensions.classification).
Transport-level failures use REST status codes: 401 with the standard REST error array (errorCode INVALID_SESSION_ID) for expired/invalid sessions.

The schema honors the context user's object- and field-level security: a field the user cannot read is absent from the schema (querying it is a ValidationError), while an accessible-but-empty field returns a field-value object whose value is null — a null value means empty data, not hidden data. This seed pins both behaviors.

Variables must travel in the variables map of the POST body — the operation document stays byte-identical across pages; only variables change (the fake rejects cursor values spliced into the query text).
